coredata.dc:title: Egg-yolk-derived carbon dots@albumin bio-nanocomposite as multifunctional coating and its application in quality maintenance of fresh litchi fruit during storage
coredata.dc:creator: Su, Xianjie
Lin, Hetong
Fu, Bofei
Mei, Shanshan
Lin, Mengshi
Chen, Hongbin
Zheng, Zongping
Bo, Hangyu
Yang, Da-Peng
Lin, Yifen
coredata.dc:description: Green synthesis of a bio-nanocomposite coating containing egg albumin, chitosan, and carbon dots (CDs) was achieved. Physical, chemical and biological properties of the coating and its application in quality maintenance of litchi were explored. The results demonstrated that the coating prepared with egg albumin, chitosan and CDs in a volume ratio of 5:5:1 had good flexibility, antibacterial, antioxidant and UV barrier properties. Compared to control litchi, during storage, the litchi coated with bio-nanocomposite displayed a lower pericarp browning index, a lower percentage of weight loss, and a lower content of reducing sugar, but retained high contents of vitamin C, total sugar, and sucrose, higher levels of chromaticity a∗, b∗ , and L∗ values. These results indicated that the novel multifunctional bio-nanocomposite coating based on egg albumin, chitosan, and CDs could be a safe and eco-friendly method to maintain the quality and reduce postharvest losses of fresh litchi during storage.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (CDs)
Albumin
Chitosan
Antioxidant
Bio-nanocomposite
Multifunctional coating
Litchi fruit
Quality maintenance

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of albumin-based composite coatings (EA, EA/CS, EA/CS/CDs coating)
Thickness and transparency of the coating
Application of EA/CS/CDs coating solution on the preservation of litchi
Measurement of the fruit pericarp browning index
Measurement of total sugar, reducing sugar, sucrose content of litchi fruit
Transparency, thickness, and appearance of the coating
Application of EA/CS/CDs coating solution in quality maintenance of postharvest fresh litchi
Vitamin C, total sugar, reducing sugar, and sucrose

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are very grateful to the Orientation project sponsored by the Fujian Provincial Department of Science and Technology, China (Grant Nos. 2019H0023 and KLe16H01A), the Science and Technology Innovation Foundation at Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University of China (Grant No. CXZX2020115A), the Quanzhou City Science & Technology Program of China (Grant No. 2017G023) and 2018 “Harbour Plan” to Introduce High-Level Talent Team Project of Quanzhou, China (Grant No. 2018CT004).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A. XPS full spectrum of CDs; B-D. High-resolution XPS spectrum of CDs including C 1 s (B), N 1 s (C) and O 1 s (D); E. FT-IR spectrum of CDs; F. XRD spectrum of CDs.

A. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrum, fluorescence excitation (EX) spectrum, and emission (EM) spectrum of CDs, and the inset figure represented under the stimulation of sunlight and 365 nm ultraviolet light stimulation; B. Emission spectrum at 340 nm to 420 nm of CDs; C. TEM image of CDs, and the inset figure showed the distribution of particle diameter; D-E. Antibacterial effect of CDs on E. coli (D) and S. aureus (E), the control group was distilled water; F. UV–vis pattern of EA, EA/CS and EA/CS /CDs coating.

A. SEM pattern of cross-section of EA/CS/CDs coating; B. EA/CS/CDs coating dried at 37 °C for 18 h under the fluorescent lamp; C. Image of EA/CS/CDs coating under 365 nm UV lamp; D. SEM micrograph of EA/CS/CDs coating; E. Energy dispersive spectra (EDS) of EA/CS/CDs coating; F-H. Element distribution map of carbon (F), oxygen (G), and nitrogen (H) in EA/CS/CDs coating.

FT-IR spectra (A), XRD spectra (B), water vapor transmission rate (WVTR) (C), and mechanical properties including tensile strength (TS) and elongation at break (EB) (D) of EA, EA/CS and EA/CS/CDs coatings.

Antibacterial effects of EA, EA/CS and EA/CS/CDs coatings on E. coli (A) and S. aureus (B); Antibacterial effects of EA, EA/CS and EA/CS/CDs coatings solution on E. coli (C) and S. aureus (D); Appearance changes of the control litchis and the EA/CH/CDs coatings solution treated-litchis during storage at 25 ± 1 °C and 85–90 % RH for 6 d (E).

Effects of EA/CS/CDs coating solution on pericarp color characteristics including L* value (A), a* value (B), and b* value (C), pulp total soluble sugar content (D), sucrose content (E), reducing sugar content (F), vitamin C content (G), fruit weight loss percent (H), and pericarp browning index (I) in litchis during storage at 25 ± 1 °C and 85–90 % RH for 6 d.

head.ce:title: Egg-yolk-derived carbon dots@albumin bio-nanocomposite as multifunctional coating and its application in quality maintenance of fresh litchi fruit during storage

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Postharvest Technology of Agricultural Products

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Green synthesis of a bio-nanocomposite coating containing egg albumin, chitosan, and carbon dots (CDs) was achieved. Physical, chemical and biological properties of the coating and its application in quality maintenance of litchi were explored. The results demonstrated that the coating prepared with egg albumin, chitosan and CDs in a volume ratio of 5:5:1 had good flexibility, antibacterial, antioxidant and UV barrier properties. Compared to control litchi, during storage, the litchi coated with bio-nanocomposite displayed a lower pericarp browning index, a lower percentage of weight loss, and a lower content of reducing sugar, but retained high contents of vitamin C, total sugar, and sucrose, higher levels of chromaticity a∗, b∗ , and L∗ values. These results indicated that the novel multifunctional bio-nanocomposite coating based on egg albumin, chitosan, and CDs could be a safe and eco-friendly method to maintain the quality and reduce postharvest losses of fresh litchi during storage.

ce:keyword.ce:text: American Society of Testing and Materials

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The authors do not have permission to share data.

ce:section.ce:para: Every year, many spoiled fruits are discarded due to the occurrence of mildew, decay and other problems, which could be caused by lipid oxidation and microbial infection. Besides, with increasing requirements for the freshness and quality of fruits by consumers, the demand for fruit preservation technology is becoming increasingly (Deng et al., 2021; Li, Liu, Zhang, Meng, & Wang, 2021). Current methods for fruit preservation mainly include modified atmosphere packaging, food irradiation, chemical preservation, etc. Although modified atmosphere packaging is widely used, it is not suitable for all fruits because the storage conditions vary significantly among different fruits. Furthermore, the fruits after irradiation show the poor storage stability. Additionally, chemical preservation may easily contaminate fruits with harmful substances (Mostafavi, Mirmajlessi, Mirjalili, Fathollahi, & Askari, 2012). Thus, having green, sustainable, and cost-effective food packaging materials is of great importance for the food industry. Recently, coatings have become the hotspot of researchers due to their excellent biocompatibility, biodegradability and cost effectiveness (Elsabee & Abdou, 2013; Mei et al., 2022). However, coatings made of a single component have certain limitations. For example, polysaccharide-based coatings have poor mechanical strength. Protein-based coatings have poor water-resistance properties. Lipids-based coatings have poor tensile strength and elongation at break. Considering the above problems, we developed a novel coating using egg white, chitosan, and carbon dots as the primary materials to achieve multifunction, such as UV blocking, antibacterial, and anti-oxidation.

Egg albumin (EA) is widely available, inexpensive, safe, biocompatible, and amphiphilic reproducible. EA has excellent functions for emulsification, foaming, gelation, and coating formation (Peng et al., 2017). Nevertheless, due to its weak water-resistance and antibacterial properties, the direct utilization of EA as a matrix for food packaging is often restricted. Therefore, many studies have modified EA to improve its properties by chemical-crosslinking (Godiya, Sayed, Xiao, & Lu, 2020), nano-reinforcing (Sabouri, Akbari, Hosseini, Khatami, & Darroudi, 2020), or mixing with other biopolymers (Soo & Sarbon, 2018). On the other hand, chitosan (CS), a deacetylated derivative of chitin with positive charges, has the characteristics of being non-toxic, biodegradable, biocompatible, and has significant antimicrobial properties (Sahariah & Masson, 2017). Thus, CS is an ideal material for fabricating an albumin-based edible coating.

Moreover, due to the shortcomings of natural antioxidants, such as being expensive and are difficult to store, a facile and green method to synthesize the coating containing antioxidants is urgently needed. Carbon dots (CDs) are a new type of carbon nanomaterial with a size <10 nm. CDs, as synthesized by biological materials, have the characteristics of low toxicity, good biocompatibility, high optical stability, amphiphilicity, high antioxidant performance, and UV shielding function (Fan, Zhang, Bhandari, & Yang, 2020). Currently, it has become a trend to use “green” materials, like egg-white (Zhang, Sun, & Wu, 2015), orange peel (Hu, Li, & Xu, 2021) and silk fibroin (Gao, Teng, & Huang, 2017), as carbon sources to synthesize CDs. CDs are now widely used in bio-imaging, biomedicines, and optoelectronics, while the use of CDs in the food packaging field is still in the early stage.

In this work, EA was used as a based matrix to provide good stability for the multifunctional composite coating. The addition of glycerol enhanced the plasticity of the coatings and improved the comprehensive properties of the edible coatings. This work aimed to develop multifunctional composite coating via the combination of CS with good antibacterial properties, EA with excellent biocompatibility, and CDs with antioxidant properties. Furthermore, according to a bionic strategy, litchi fruit was soaked in or smeared with this albumin-based multifunctional composite coating solution to form a protective layer on the fruit surface. This layer can keep the invaders out, such as water, oxygen, and microorganisms. The preservation effects and feasibility of using the coating as a technical guidance for harvested fruit were also assessed.

Eggs were obtained from Xinhuadu Supercenter Co., ltd. (Quanzhou, China). Chitosan with 80–95 % deacetylation glycerin, and DPPH (96 %) was bought from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., ltd. (Shanghai, China). Acetic acid (100 %) was bought from Xilong Chemical Co., ltd. (Guangzhou, China). In addition, ABTS (98 %) was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Co., ltd. (St. Louis, Missouri, United States). Escherichia coli ATCC 8739 and Staphylococcus aureus ATCC 6538 were acquired from the Second Affiliated Hospital, Fujian Medical University (Quanzhou, China).

After the separation from egg white, egg yolk was put into a reaction kettle. Follow by conducting at 220 °C for 24 h, the solution was centrifuged at 15 000 r/min for 20 min to eliminate the insoluble matter. After centrifugation, the supernatant was collected, then the collected supernatant was filtrated using a 0.22 μm filter head. Hereafter, the dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 3500 Da was adopted to dialyze the filtered liquid samples in ultrapure water for 48 h. The obtained sample was defined as CDs.

The surface elemental compositions and chemical bonding configurations of CDs were recorded using XPS (Kratos Axis Ultra DLD, Sweden). FT-IR spectra of the samples were detected using FT-IR spectrometer (Model Nicolet iS50, Thermo Scientific, United States). XRD patterns of samples were examined using X-ray diffractometer (Model D8 Advance, Bruker AXS GmbH, Bruker corporation, Karlsruhe, Germany) with Cu Kα radiation. UV–vis spectra of CDs were detected using UV–vis spectrophotometer (Model Specord S600, Analytik Jena AG, Germany). Fluorescence spectra of CDs were obtained using a photoluminescent spectrophotometer (Model FL970, Tianmei Scientific Instrument Co., ltd., Shanghai, China). TEM images of samples were recorded using transmission electron microscope (Model Tecnai F30, Philips-FEI, The Netherlands) with a 300 kV working voltage. The filter paper method was used to evaluate the activity of CDs against E. coli and S. aureus.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of albumin-based composite coatings (EA, EA/CS, EA/CS/CDs coating)
ce:section.ce:para: First, the EA coating solution was prepared by diluting raw egg albumin separated from eggs in 1.5 times water and the addition of glycerin. The final concentration of the mixed solution was 1.5 %. Then, CS solution (1.5 %, w/v) was dissolved in 100 mL acetic acid (1 %, v/v) and then mixed with EA solution in equal proportions. This mixed solution was named as EA/CS coating solution after stirring for 20 min. Subsequently, the EA/CS coating solution was mixed with the CDs solution in a volume ratio of 10:1 to obtain the mixture coating solution, namely, EA/CS/CDs coating solution, i.e., the coating solution of EA/CS/CDs was prepared with egg albumin, chitosan and CDs in a volume ratio of 5:5:1. Lastly, these three kinds of solution (EA, EA/CS, and EA/CS/CDs) were dried at 40 °C for 12–16 h to obtain the corresponding coating, separately.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Thickness and transparency of the coating
ce:section.ce:para: Three different positions of each kind of sample were selected to measure the thickness of the albumin-based composite coating by a digital display outside micrometer (Guangdong, China) with an accuracy of 0.001 mm.

Also, the coating was cut into a strip roughly equaled to 1 cm × 3 cm. Referring to the approach of Roy & Rhim (2020), a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Model Specord S600, Analytik Jena AG, Germany) was used to assess the transparency of the strip through assaying light transmittance at 660 nm (T660) and 280 nm (T280).

The albumin-based composite coatings were analyzed by FT-IR spectrometer (Model Nicolet iS50, Thermo Scientific, United States). The resolution of FT-IR spectrometer is 4 cm−1, and the range is 650–4000 cm−1.
The pieces with 40 mm × 40 mm, cutting from the albumin-based composite coatings, were used for the analysis of XRD patterns applying a Cu Kα radiation source. The scanning speed was 5°/min.

WVTR was analyzed by a slightly modified ASTM method of Fu et al. (2021). The coating sample was fixed in a bottle with a mouth diameter of 12 mm containing 10 mL of ultrapure water. The bottle was placed in the incubator with a constant RH (50 ± 2 %) and temperature (37 ± 0.5 °C) for a maximum of 10 h, and it was weighed every hour.

The coatings' mechanical properties were tested on a universal tensile machine (Model Instron 3369, Instron Corporation, United States). In accordance with the ASTM D882-09 Standard Test Method, the coating sample, which was previously placed at room temperature, was cut into 10 mm × 80 mm strips. In addition, the clamping distance was controlled to be about 50 mm and the stretching speed of the stretcher was set to 50 mm/min.

The surface and internal morphology of the albumin-based composite coatings were characterized by SEM (Zeiss, Oberkochen, Germany) at 8 kV accelerating voltage. The coatings were cut into small pieces (10 mm × 10 mm) before the experiment. Subsequently, the small pieces of coatings were covered with gold in an Emitech metallizer for 2 min, and the morphology was recorded. At the same time, EDS detectors were used to detect the type, amount, and distribution of coating elements.

According to the method introduced by Munhoz, Moreira, & Bresolin (2018) with slight modification, the albumin-based composite coatings were cut into the discs with 3 cm diameter and then covered on an agar plate spread with S. aureus and E. coli. Additionally, the antimicrobial activities of the EA/CS/CDs coating solution against S. aureus and E. coli were determined using the method of filter paper, and the EA coating solution was used as the control. The filter paper was immersed in the sample solution with sterilizing forceps, placed on the plates smeared with S. aureus and E. coli, and gently pressed down with forceps. Afterwards, the plates were sealed with a para-film, then were inverted and incubated at 37 °C for 12 h.

(1) FRAP: The FRAP of the EA/CS/CDs coating was determined using the slightly modified approach of Zareie, Yazdi, & Mortazavi (2020). The EA/CS/CDs coating solution (1 mL) was mixed with 2.5 mL of ultrapure water and 2.5 mL of 1 % (w/v) potassium ferricyanide, then the mixed solution was incubated for 10 min at 50 °C before being added to 2.5 mL of 10 % (w/v) trichloroacetic acid. The above mixture was centrifuged for 10 min at 4000 r/min, and the supernatant was collected for the following assay. 2.5 mL of the above supernatant was mixed with 2.5 mL of ultrapure water and 0.5 mL of 0.1 % (w/v) ferric chloride, then the mixture was kept for 10 min at room temperature and dark condition, hereafter, the absorbance of the resultant mixture was tested at 700 nm.

(2) DPPH• scavenging activity: The slightly modified approach of Liang et al. (2017) was used to measure the DPPH• scavenging activity using a microplate reader (Infinite M200 Pro, Switzerland). The sample of EA/CS/CDs coating solution (0.1 mL) was admixed with 2.9 mL of 0.15 mM DPPH solution (diluted with 95 % ethanol), hereafter, the mixed mixture was placed for 30 min at room temperature and dark condition, and then was centrifuged for 5 min at 8 000 r/min, afterwards, the supernatant was collected, and the absorbance of supernatant was measured at 517 nm. The DPPH• scavenging activity was calculated based on the following formula.
DPPH• scavenging activity (%) = 100 × (A blank-A sample)/A blank.
Where A blank and A sample were the absorbance of ultrapure water and the coating solution sample, respectively.

(3) ABTS•+ scavenging activity: The assay of ABTS•+ scavenging activity was conducted using the modified method of Dudonne, Vitrac, & Coutiere (2009). The solution of 7.0 mM ABTS was mixed with the solution of 2.45 mM of persulphuric acid potassium in a ratio of 2:1 (v/v), then the mixed solution was placed at room temperature and dark condition for 12–16 h. Hereafter, the above mixed solution was diluted with ultrapure water until its absorbance at 734 nm became 0.70 ± 0.05 to yield the ABTS working solution. The EA/CS/CDs coating solution (0.05 mL) was mixed with 3.95 mL of ABTS working solution, then the mixture was kept for 20 min at room temperature and dark condition, hereafter, the absorbance of the mixture was measured at 734 nm. The ABTS•+ scavenging activity was computed using the following formula.
ABTS•+ scavenging activity (%) = 100 × (A blank-A sample)/A blank.
Where A blank and A sample were the absorbance of ultrapure water and the coating solution sample, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of EA/CS/CDs coating solution on the preservation of litchi
ce:section.ce:para: ‘Wuye’ litchi fruit were harvested from the Nanjin Forest Farm in Nanan City, Fujian Province. The values of litchi fruit appearance chromaticity L*, a*, b* were 49.37 ± 0.97, 25.78 ± 0.79 and 25.39 ± 0.90, respectively. Litchis were selected for this experiment with roughly uniform in appearance, size, and color, but no physical damage, free of pests and diseases.

All the selected litchis (N = 1350) were rinsed with distilled water. A total of 90 rinsed litchis were used for measuring the parameters at harvesting day. The remaining 1260 rinsed litchis were divided into two groups. One group of litchis (630 fruits) were soaked in ultrapure water for 5 min (the control group), the other group of litchis (630 fruits) were immersed in EA/CS/CDs coating solution for 5 min (EA/CS/CDs group). Hereafter, the above two groups of treated-litchis were air-dried naturally at room temperature for 1 h, then were separately packaged in polyethylene film bag (30 litchis in each bag, 21 bags per group), and stored at 25 ± 1 °C and 85–90 % RH. During storage, three bags (90 litchis in total) from each group were sampled daily for the following determination. Additionally, three bags (90 litchis in total) were selected from each group and used to regularly measure the weight loss of fruit during the entire storage period.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of the fruit pericarp browning index
ce:section.ce:para: The slightly modified protocol of Jiang et al. (2018) was chosen to measure the browning index of litchi fruit. Thirty litchi fruits were selected every day and their pericarp browning was observed. The browning of 30 litchi pericarps randomly selected are graded, and the litchi pericarp index is calculated according to the following formula.
The browning index of litchi pericarp = ∑ (browning index × number of fruits of this grade)/ total fruits.

Referring to the approaches of Chen et al. (2020) and Jiang et al. (2018), four spots on the opposite side of the equatorial plane of 10 individual litchis were applied for assaying chromaticity L*, a* and b* values in litchi fruit appearance using a Chroma meter (CR- 400, Konica Minolta, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of total sugar, reducing sugar, sucrose content of litchi fruit
ce:section.ce:para: The total sugar, reducing sugar, and sucrose content of postharvest litchis were measured based on the approaches of Chen et al. (2020) and Jiang et al. (2018). The results were expressed in g kg−1.
According to the approach of Jiang et al. (2018), 2.0 g of litchi pulp was taken from 10 fruits to detect vitamin C content. The results were expressed in g kg−1.

Based on the approaches of Jiang et al. (2018) and Lin et al. (2020), three bags of litchis were used to regularly assay the weight loss of fruit during storage. The weight loss percent, compared to the initial weight, was calculated according to the following formula.
W e i g h t
l o s s
p e r c e n t
=
(
i n i t i a l
w e i g h t - d a i l y
w e i g h t )
i n i t i a l
w e i g h t
× 100 %

Evaluations of all the above parameters were performed three replicates. SPSS 21 software and Duncan’s multiple comparison analysis were employed, and the significant differences were identified at a remarkable level of 0.05 (p < 0.05).

The XPS survey spectrum indicated that the characteristic peaks of C, N, and O elements were located at 284.8 eV, 399.7 eV, and 531.2 eV with atomic concentrations of 57.8 %, 11.2 %, and 29.2 %, separately (Fig. 1 A). The high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s showed three characteristic peaks at 288.1 eV, 286.3 eV, and 284.8 eV, suggesting the presence of CO, CO, and CC, separately (Fig. 1B) (Sun et al., 2021). The high-resolution scan of the N 1 s indicated the presence of C-NH2 and C-(NH)-C at 399.7 eV and 401.7 eV, respectively (Fig. 1C). Additionally, the high-resolution spectrum of O 1 s exhibited two peaked at 531.2 eV and 532.5 eV, which might be attributed to the CO and CO, separately (Fig. 1D). The FT-IR spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1E) showed the existence of CH and OH groups at 2957 cm−1 and 3241 cm−1, separately, which made CDs hydrophilic and improved their stability and dispersibility in water (Shen, Shang, Chen, Wang, & Cai, 2017). The typical absorption at 1235 cm−1 was owing to the NH stretching vibration. The peak at 1668 cm−1 was due to CO groups, and the existence of peaked at 1045 cm−1 and 1111 cm−1 were attributed to the COC bond. These results indicated that CDs contained sp2 carbons and sp3 hybrid carbons (Zhao et al., 2017). The XRD spectra of CDs (Fig. 1F) showed a maximum (003) peak near 24.08°. Through JADE 6.0 software analysis, the pattern of the CDs samples matched well with the existing nitrogen oxide graphite structure crystals. This result agreed with a previous report that the graphene structure crystals appeared around 18.42°–25.92° (Tang et al., 2012).

UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired to further study the CDs’ optical properties. In Fig. 2A, a weak peak was observed at 275 nm, which might be due to the n-π* transition of CO and the π-π* transition of CC*. In addition, CDs emission was observed at 408 nm when it was excited at 332 nm. Moreover, the emission of CDs was observed between the wavelength of 340 nm to 420 nm, and the highest emission was observed at 340 nm (Fig. 2 B). Fig. 2C showed that CDs were evenly dispersed in the copper mesh. In addition, the histogram (inset) about the diameter distribution of corresponding particles showed that the particle diameters of all CDs ranged from 1 nm to 6 nm with the average diameter of 2.04 nm. This result indicated that CDs were successfully synthesized.

An appropriate combination of antibacterial materials can enable strong antibacterial activities. Fig. S1 displayed that the zeta potential of the synthesized CDs was about +10.5 mV. The cell walls of S. aureus and E. coli were negatively charged (Sun et al., 2021). Thus, positively charged CDs could be adsorbed on the bacterial surface by electrostatic attraction. In addition, FT-IR and XPS analyses of CDs demonstrate that CDs had oxygen-containing clusters, such as CO and CO. These oxygen-containing clusters could make CDs bind well to bacterial cell wall and cell membrane, thereby destroying the cell structure and killing bacteria. That’s why CDs exhibited antibacterial activities against S. aureus and E. coli (Fig. 2D, 2E).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Transparency, thickness, and appearance of the coating
ce:section.ce:para: Ultraviolet spectra, whose typical wavelength was from 10 nm to 400 nm, could be divided into UV-C (100 nm − 280 nm), UV-B (280 nm − 315 nm), and UV-A (315 nm − 400 nm). Fig. 2F showed that the UV transmittance of EA/CS/CDs coating was extremely low in the range of UV-C, UV-B, and UV-A, compared with EA and EA/CS coating, which might be attributed to the special UV obstruction function of the EA/CS/CDs coating. Also, Fig. 2F showed that the EA/CS/CDs coating could absorb the high-energy blue light (HEBL), ranging from 400 nm − 450 nm, which was harmful to the fruit and the human body. In addition, the Duncan’s multiple range tests indicate that the transparency of EA, EA/CS, and EA/CS/CDs coating was not considerably different at T280 and T660 (p > 0.05). What’s more, the T660 of EA, EA/CS, and EA/CS/CDs coating were 81.98 %, 90.84 %, and 85.61 %, respectively (Table S1). The results showed that the addition of CDs could impart antioxidant property to the EA/CS coating, and CDs has little effect on the visible light transmittance of the coating (p > 0.05).

As Table S1 showed, the EA/CS/CDs coating was significantly thinner than the EA coating in the same volume, which might be due to closer interaction between albumin and CS (p < 0.05). At the same time, the EA/CS/CDs coating was a little thicker than the EA/CS coating. Furthermore, the EA/CS/CDs coating showed a light yellow and transparent under sunlight (Fig. 3 B), but it emitted blue and pH-stable fluorescence (Fig. S2) under 365 nm ultraviolet light (Fig. 3C). Further, with the increasing added number of CDs, the blue fluorescence brightness of the EA/CS/CDs coating also gradually increased (Fig. S3). This fluorescence phenomenon made EA/CS/CDs coating an anti-counterfeiting function.

The SEM patterns of surface (Fig. 3A) and cross-section (Fig. 4 D) of EA/CS/CDs coating were flat, compact with complete structure, which could improve the porous structure of the EA coating (Fig. S4A, S4D) and fill short pits in the EA/CS coating (Fig. S4B, S4E). This result was consistent with Fu et al. (2021) who found that adding chitosan could eliminate the loose structure of discarded fish gelatin films, thereby providing a more compact cross-section. Konwar, Gogoi, Majumdar, & Chowdhury (2015) also supported the addition of CDs to the chitosan hydrogel matrix, which contributed to a smooth membrane surface due to the properties of CDs as fillers. In addition, as shown in Fig. 3E, the EDS surface scan analysis of the coating indicated that the EA/CS/CDs coating was primarily consisted of the elements of carbon (C), oxygen (O), and nitrogen (N).

The changes in chemical interactions within albumin-based composite coatings were analyzed by FT-IR (Fig. 4A). The EA, EA/CS, and EA/CS/CDs coatings had characteristic peaks at ∼ 3295 cm−1 (NH tensile vibration, amide A), 2931 cm−1 (CH and NH3 + asymmetric tensile vibration (amide B), 1640 cm−1 (CO stretching of COO group (amide-I), 1539 cm−1 (the fluctuation pattern due to a heterogeneous combination of CN stretching of the peptide group of 1550 cm−1 (amide-II) and in-plane NH deformation patterns). Liu et al. (2012) reported similar results of gelatin coatings, in which amide-A, amide-Ⅰ, and amide-Ⅱ peaks were observed at the wavelengths of 3299 cm−1, 1656 cm−1, and 1550 cm−1, separately. In addition, the weak peak of the EA coating at 2931 cm−1 might be caused by CH bond vibrations in the –CH3 and –CH2 functional groups. When the CS solution and CDs solution were successively added to the EA coating solution, the CH bond positions in EA and EA/CS coating were successively shifted to 2934.4 cm−1 and 2925.45 cm−1, respectively. This result might be due to the albumin de-saturated effect caused by the interaction of EA and CS, which might reduce the hydrogen bond number. Moreover, similar research had shown that the infrared wavelength of tensile vibrations could be reduced by hydrogen bonding (Barth, 2007).

The XRD patterns showed that the albumin-based composite coatings were semi-crystalline polymers (Fig. 4B). The peak width and strength of EA coating were lower than those of the EA/CS coating and EA/CS/CDs coating, indicating that the amorphous form of EA coating was more severe than the other two coatings. This phenomenon might be related to the hydrophilicity of albumin and the stronger absorbability of the binder (Ai, Xiao, & Jiang, 2021). Leceta, Arana, Guerrero, & Caba (2014) also found that, with more glycerin added, the crystallinity of the glycerin chitosan mixture decreased, which subsequently led to higher moisture absorption. Additionally, all different albumin-based composite coating samples had three reflection peaks. For the EA coating, the peak values were 20.06°, 30.29°, and 41.03°, respectively. The peak values of the EA/CS coating were 13.8°, 30.4°, and 41.5°, respectively. For the EA/CS/CDs coating, the peak values were 14.29°, 2.98°, and 41.15°, respectively. The peak value at about 30.4° of the EA coating was slightly lower than the EA/CS and EA/CS/CDs coating. The change in protein crystallization behavior was essentially related to glycosylation, the way monosaccharides bind to proteins, the molecular forces that affected recrystallization, and the crystal orientation of proteins (Ai et al. 2021). Moreover, CDs loosened the EA/CS/CDs coating, causing the hydrogen bond to be impeded and educating crystallinity, which was consistent with that of Liu, Cai, Jiang, Wu, & Le (2016).

Fig. 4C showed the WVTR of albumin-based composite coatings. The WVTR values of the EA/CS coating and EA/CS/CDs coating were 193.36 and 204.49 g·mm/(m2·day), separately, which were considerably lower than EA coating (p < 0.01). Additionally, compared to EA/CS coating, the clearly higher WVTR was found in EA/CS/CDs coating (p < 0.05). This result might be due to the addition of CDs to the EA/CS coating, making the coating thicker. In other words, a relatively low level of WVTR maintained in the EA/CS coating might be due to its more compact and denser EA/CS coating microstructure because of the interaction between EA and CS. Davoodi et al. (2020) indicated that chitosan was hydrophobic, the WVTR of Salvia macrosiphon / chitosan coatings decreased with the increase of the chitosan content.

Good mechanical capacity of the coating were required to ensure that the fruit was protected by the coating during the processing, transportation, sale, and storage. The tests of TS and EB were applied to evaluate the mechanical capacity of the coating. The mechanical capacity of the albumin-based coatings was shown in Fig. 4D. Compared with the values of TS and EB in the EA coating, those in the EA/CS coating were at significantly higher levels (p < 0.05). It indicated that adding chitosan significantly increased the TS and EB of the EA coating (p < 0.05). Moreover, compared with the EA/CS coating, the addition of CDs significantly reduced the TS level (p < 0.05), but slightly increased the EB level of the EA/CS/CDs coating. As shown in the scanning electron microscopy results (Fig. 3D), the addition of CDs nanoparticles enabled excellent compatibility and structure stability of the EA/CS coating, so the EB of the EA/CS/CDs coating increased. Ezati, Riahi, & Rhim (2021) also reported that incorporating nanoparticles into a polymer matrix would result in a more compact structure and better mechanical properties.

Fig. 5 A and Fig. 5B showed that the area covered by the EA coating was overgrown with E. coli and S. aureus bacteria, while there was no bacterial growth in the coating range covered by the EA/CS and EA/CS/CDs coatings. The existence of a bacteriostatic ring proved that the solution had a bacteriostatic effect (Ge et al., 2018). As shown in Fig. 5C and Fig. 5D, the EA coating solution had no antibacterial ring. In contrast, EA/CS/CDs coating had a large antibacterial ring. These results indicated that the EA coating solution had no antibacterial effect or a relatively weak antibacterial effect. In contrast, the EA/CS/CDs coating solution had stronger antibacterial effects on S. aureus and E. coli. The antibacterial effects of the EA/CS and EA/CS/CDs coatings were mainly attributed to the antibacterial effect of macromolecular chitosan, which could prevent the transport of nutrients to the microbial cell by forming a layer of molecular membrane on their surface that played a bacteriostatic role. Additionally, the interaction between of chitosan with positive charges and the surface of microbial cell membrane with negative charges could alter the membrane permeability of microbial cells, and eventually kill microbial cells.

The above results demonstrated that, compared with the EA coating and EA/CS coating, EA/CS/CDs coating had UV shielding function and excellent antioxidant properties. Therefore, the EA/CS/CDs coating was chosen to study antioxidant activity and fruit preservation. The antioxidant activities of EA/CS/CDs coating, with different addition amounts of CDs at 0, 2 %, 4 %, 6 %, 8 %, 10 %, and 12 %, were measured using FRAP, DPPH, and ABTS assays and the data were shown in Table S2. The results of FRAP and ABTS assays differed significantly among different EA/CS/CDs coatings with different adding amounts of CDs at 0, 2 %, 4 %, 6 %, 8 %, and 10 % (p < 0.05), while FRAP assay showed no differences between EA/CS/CDs-10 % and EA/CS/CDs-12 % coatings (p > 0.05). The DPPH assays showed no differences among different EA/CS/CDs coatings with the adding amounts of CDs at 0, 2 %, and 4 % (p > 0.05), while they differed significantly among different EA/CS/CDs coatings with adding amounts of CDs at 6 %, 8 %, and 12 % (p < 0.05). The FRAP, DPPH, and ABTS assays showed that the antioxidant activity of the coating increased with the addition of the CDs solution, except for the DPPH• scavenging activity of the EA/CS/CDs coating with 12 % CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of EA/CS/CDs coating solution in quality maintenance of postharvest fresh litchi

ce:section.ce:para: The L* value mainly reflects the brightness of the litchi pericarp. When a* and b* values are positive, a* denotes a red color and b* indicates a yellow color (Chen et al., 2020). As shown in Fig. 6 A-6C, the values of L*, a*, and b* of litchi fruit gradually decreased with the prolongation of storage days. During storage days 1–6, the values of L*, a*, and b* in the EA/CS/CDs group were maintained at a higher level than the control group. These results showed that the EA/CS/CDs coating solution could effectively maintain the brightness and red color of litchi, thereby retaining higher quality of litchi fruit. In addition, this conclusion could also be further confirmed through observing the changes of appearance color in litchi fruit during storage (Fig. 5D).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Vitamin C, total sugar, reducing sugar, and sucrose
ce:section.ce:para: Vitamin C, total sugar, reducing sugar, and sucrose are the principal indicators to evaluate nutritive quality of fresh produces (Chen et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2022; Fan et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2017). In present work, during storage, total sugar (Fig. 6D), sucrose (Fig. 6E), and vitamin C (Fig. 6G) in litchi fruit showed a downward trend, but reducing sugar (Fig. 6F) exhibited an upward trend. Compared to the control group, the EA/CS/CDs group displayed a considerably (p < 0.05) higher level of total sugar (Fig. 6D), sucrose (Fig. 6E), and vitamin C (Fig. 6G) at storage days 2–6, days 1–6, and days 1–6, respectively, but manifested a clearly lower level of reducing sugar at days 1–5 (p < 0.05). These data suggested that the EA/CS/CDs coating solution could effectively restrain the hydrolysis of sucrose to reducing sugar including glucose and fructose, and maintain higher level of nutritive quality including vitamin C, sucrose, and total sugar in fresh litchis during storage.

Weight loss percent is an important indicator for assessing the storability of fresh produces (Chen et al., 2020; Chen et al., 2022; Fan et al., 2022; Liu et al., 2021; Wu et al., 2017). Fig. 6H showed that, during storage, the weight loss percent in fresh litchis displayed an upward trend. Additionally, the lower level of weight loss from fresh litchis was found in the EA/CS/CDs group than the control group during storage days 1–6. Moreover, compared to the control group, the EA/CS/CDs group displayed a considerably lower weight loss percent at days 3–6 (p < 0.05). These data suggested that the EA/CS/CDs coating solution could reduce weight loss in fresh litchis during storage, and thus improve the storability of fresh litchis. The above findings were in accordance with the work of Jung, Cui, & Barnes (2020), who reported that, the application of albumin composite coating solution could lower the percent of weight loss in fresh strawberries during storage.

Fig. 6I showed that, pericarp browning index of litchi fruit raised with the prolongation of storage days. At storage days 3–6, the pericarp of control litchis displayed severe browning. Additionally, a clearly lower pericarp browning index was found in the EA/CS/CDs group than the control group during storage days 3–6 (p < 0.05). For example, on storage day 6, pericarp browning index of the control litchis was 4.59, while pericarp browning index of the litchis treated with EA/CS/CDs coating solution was only 3.32. These results indicated that the EA/CS/CDs coating solution could effectively delay pericarp browning of fresh litchis during storage.

In conclusion, a green, sustainable, and cost-effective multifunctional bio-nanocomposite coating was successfully developed using eggs, chitosan, and CDs. The main raw material came from egg albumin, which was cheap and easy to obtain. Thereby it could effectively reduce the cost of packaging materials. The prepared multifunctional bio-nanocomposite coating with egg albumin, chitosan and CDs in a volume ratio of 5:5:1 had excellent physical and chemical properties, such as flexibility, UV blocking, antibacterial, and antioxidant activities. SEM and EDS analyses showed that CDs had good compatibility and could be evenly distributed on the surface of the EA/CS/CDs multifunctional coating, thereby maintaining a flat, compact, and complete matrix network structure of the coatings. In addition, FT-IR spectra showed that there were CH3, CO, and NH bonds in the EA/CS/CDs coating, indicating the excellent stability of the coating. Moreover, the application of bio-nanocomposite materials such as EA/CS/CDs coating solution could effectively maintained the quality of postharvest fresh litchis, including appearance color, total pulp sugar, sucrose, and vitamin C content. Although the addition of CDs reduced the TS of the EA/CS/CDs coating, it increased the flexibility of the coating. Therefore, the EA/CS/CDs coating prepared in this work had excellent antibacterial and antioxidant properties, providing a new method for litchi fruit preservation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are very grateful to the Orientation project sponsored by the Fujian Provincial Department of Science and Technology, China (Grant Nos. 2019H0023 and KLe16H01A), the Science and Technology Innovation Foundation at Fujian Agriculture and Forestry University of China (Grant No. CXZX2020115A), the Quanzhou City Science & Technology Program of China (Grant No. 2017G023) and 2018 “Harbour Plan” to Introduce High-Level Talent Team Project of Quanzhou, China (Grant No. 2018CT004).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.foodchem.2022.134813.
